finding.summary: The AES key material stored in key_big (sourced from key_in/key_reg_out[0]) is fully readable back through the external register bus interface without any access control or privilege check, allowing any bus master to extract the 192-bit AES key via register reads at addresses 16–21.

evidence.description: rom2 stores cryptographic key material in secure_reg. The 0th location (192'h55555555_28aed2a6_abf71588_09cf4f3c_2b7e1516_28aed2a6) is labeled as the AES key. This is sensitive material intended to be protected.
evidence.supports_claim: key_big/key_in contains a secret AES-192 key loaded from the ROM key store.

evidence.description: rom2 copies mem[] into secure_reg on reset (line 34: secure_reg <= mem) and allows write access to secure_reg via the bus interface with no privilege check (line 40: secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i when req_i && we_i). There is no lock, authentication, or access-control enforcement on reads or writes to secure_reg.
evidence.supports_claim: The key register has no hardware protection against bus-level read or write access.

evidence.description: key_reg_out[0] (the AES key slot from rom2.secure_reg[0]) is passed directly as key_in to i_aes_wrapper with no gating or access-control check.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key flows unguarded from the key store into the AES wrapper.

evidence.description: In the read-side always @(*) block of aes_wrapper (lines 128-139), register addresses 16 through 21 map directly to key_big[191:160] down to key_big[31:0] and are returned unconditionally via external_bus_io.rdata. There is no guard condition, privilege check, lock register, or access-control signal consulted before returning the key material. Any entity with read access to the external bus can retrieve the full 192-bit key.
evidence.supports_claim: The full 192-bit AES key is readable by any bus master through the unguarded register interface.

evidence.description: The access_ctrl_reg signals are derived from key_reg_out[2] and key_reg_out[3] (lines 216-217), but these access control values are never consumed within aes_wrapper or used as a gate on the key readback path. No connection between access_ctrl_reg and the aes_wrapper read path is present.
evidence.supports_claim: Although an access control register exists in the system, it is not applied to protect key readback in aes_wrapper.

finding.reasoning_summary: The AES-192 key is loaded from rom2's secure_reg[0] into aes_wrapper as key_in, where it is assigned to key_big. The read-side register map in aes_wrapper unconditionally exposes all 192 bits of key_big across six 32-bit register read addresses (16–21) via external_bus_io.rdata. No privilege check, lock signal, or access_ctrl_reg gating is applied to these read paths. The system defines access_ctrl_reg outputs but never wires them into the AES wrapper's key protection logic. Any bus master capable of issuing register reads to the AES peripheral can extract the complete key.
finding.security_impact: Full disclosure of the 192-bit AES secret key to any bus master with read access to the AES peripheral's register interface. This completely breaks the confidentiality of AES encryption performed by this module, as the key can be exfiltrated and used to decrypt any ciphertext produced or intercept future encryptions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is theoretically possible that bus-level access control upstream (e.g., in the AXI interconnect or firewall not shown) could restrict which masters can reach the AES peripheral's register space. However, no such control is visible in the provided RTL, and the aes_wrapper itself contains zero key-readback protection.

finding.summary: AES key material is readable back through the bus interface without any access control guard, exposing the full 192-bit AES key to any bus master that can read the aes_wrapper register map.

evidence.description: rom2 stores all cryptographic keys in 'mem' (const fuse replica) and copies them into 'secure_reg' on reset. secure_reg[0] (192-bit) is the AES key, secure_reg[1][31:0] is the JTAG key, and secure_reg[2..3] are access-control registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key is sourced from a fuse-backed secure register and should be treated as secret material that must not be readable by software.

evidence.description: key_reg_out[0] (the full 192-bit AES key) is wired directly to aes_wrapper's key_in port with no intervening access-control check: '.key_in (key_reg_out[0])'. key_reg_out[2][47:0] and key_reg_out[3][47:0] become access_ctrl_reg, but this control register is never consulted before exposing key_reg_out[0].
evidence.supports_claim: The permission/access-control registers exist but are not applied as a guard on the path from the secure key register to the AES wrapper.

evidence.description: In the aes_wrapper read-side combinational always block (lines 128-139), register addresses 16-21 map directly to key_big[191:0] (which equals key_in, the 192-bit AES key). Any bus master that issues a read to those addresses receives the plaintext key with no privilege or access-control check performed inside aes_wrapper or in the wrapping logic before the data reaches external_bus_io.rdata.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key is unconditionally exposed on the read-data bus at well-known register offsets (addr[6:2] == 16..21), constituting a sensitive key readback vulnerability.

evidence.description: The aes_wrapper is connected to reg_bus_aes, which is derived from the AXI 'aes' slave port exposed to bus masters. There is no access_ctrl_reg filtering applied on this AXI-to-regbus path before reads reach aes_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: Any AXI master routed to the aes peripheral address region can read back the full 192-bit key without restriction.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg is decoded into access_ctrl[i][j] per peripheral and fed into the AXI interconnect, suggesting per-peripheral access control was intended. However, there is no evidence in any snippet that this gate actually blocks read transactions to aes_wrapper's key registers once a master reaches the aes_wrapper bus interface.
evidence.supports_claim: Even if the AXI node enforces access_ctrl at the interconnect level, the key readback registers inside aes_wrapper represent a defense-in-depth failure: the key is stored in plaintext registers with no internal guard.

evidence.description: rom2 allows arbitrary writes to secure_reg via the bus interface when req_i is asserted and we_i is high (line 40: secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i). There is no authentication or privilege check inside rom2 before overwriting a key slot, including the AES key slot.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker with write access to the rom2_fuse AXI slave can overwrite the AES key (or access-control registers) to values of their choosing, completely defeating the key-management scheme.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain shows that AES key material flows from rom2.secure_reg[0] → ariane_peripherals.key_reg_out[0] → aes_wrapper.key_in → aes_wrapper.key_big. Inside aes_wrapper, the read-side register map unconditionally returns all six 32-bit slices of key_big at offsets 16–21 on external_bus_io.rdata with no privilege check. Additionally, rom2 allows unchecked writes to secure_reg, so an attacker can also inject an arbitrary key. The access_ctrl_reg mechanism exists at the AXI interconnect level but provides no protection at the register-file level within aes_wrapper.
finding.security_impact: Full disclosure of the 192-bit AES key to any bus master that can reach the aes_wrapper register space. Additionally, any master reaching the rom2_fuse AXI slave can overwrite the AES key, JTAG key, or access-control registers, subverting the entire permission hierarchy.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed whether the AXI interconnect's access_ctrl enforcement (SNIP-0008) actually prevents unprivileged masters from reaching the aes_wrapper address region in all privilege modes. If the interconnect guard is fully effective, the severity is reduced but not eliminated, because the key readback registers remain unguarded at the IP level and a privileged master (or a compromised one) would still read the key in plaintext.

finding.summary: The AES wrapper exposes the full 192-bit AES key (key_big) as readable data through the bus interface without any access control or privilege check, allowing any bus master to read back the secret key via register addresses 16-21.

evidence.description: key_big is a 192-bit internal signal derived directly from key_in (line 57: assign key_big = key_in), which represents the AES secret key used for encryption.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key is a sensitive cryptographic asset that should not be readable back through a general-purpose bus interface.

evidence.description: Lines 128-139 of the read-side always @(*) block unconditionally map key_big[191:160], key_big[159:128], key_big[127:96], key_big[95:64], key_big[63:32], and key_big[31:0] to external_bus_io.rdata at register addresses 16-21 with no privilege check, lock bit, or any guard condition. Any bus master with access to this address range can read the full 192-bit AES key.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material is directly readable via the register bus interface without any access control guard.

evidence.description: The read-side logic at lines 105-151 is a plain combinational always @(*) block with a case statement on the address. There is no privilege level check, no lock/enable register, no read-once mechanism, and no authentication before returning key material. The only condition gating reads is the address selection itself.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of any permission or access control guard on the key readback path confirms the vulnerability.

evidence.description: The external_bus_io REG_BUS interface (line 33) is a general-purpose register bus connected from ariane_peripherals. The bus ready and error signals are hardwired to 1 and 0 respectively (lines 51-52), meaning all accesses are always accepted with no error response that could block a read.
evidence.supports_claim: The bus interface is always open and ready, so nothing blocks a read of the key registers.

evidence.description: In ariane_peripherals, key_reg_out[0] (the 192-bit key from a secure ROM) is directly wired to the key_in port of i_aes_wrapper (line 467), and reg_bus_aes (the APB-to-register-bus bridge output) is connected to external_bus_io (line 468), making the key accessible to any APB bus master.
evidence.supports_claim: The instantiation confirms the key flows directly into the wrapper where it is exposed as a readable register to APB bus masters.

evidence.description: Lines 141-147 also expose inter_state (AES intermediate state) at register addresses 22-25, leaking further internal cryptographic state beyond just the key, compounding the information disclosure.
evidence.supports_claim: Both the secret key and intermediate AES computation state are readable without restriction, enabling full cryptographic key and state recovery by any bus master.

finding.reasoning_summary: The aes_wrapper module exposes AES key material (key_big, all 192 bits) and AES intermediate state (inter_state, 128 bits) through a combinational read-side register map at fixed addresses (16-21 for key, 22-25 for inter_state) with absolutely no access control, privilege check, lock bit, or any other guard. The bus interface is always ready and never signals an error. Any bus master that can address the AES peripheral register space can read back the complete secret AES key. This is a direct sensitive-data readback vulnerability with no mitigating controls present in the RTL.
finding.security_impact: Complete disclosure of the 192-bit AES secret key and 128-bit intermediate encryption state to any bus master with access to the AES peripheral address space. This allows an attacker with bus access to recover the secret key and decrypt any data encrypted with that key, fully compromising confidentiality of all AES-protected data.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet does not show the full address map or whether there is any upper-level bus firewall that might restrict access to the AES peripheral address range. However, the RTL itself contains no such protection, so any firewall would be external and architectural rather than a property of this module. The key readback vulnerability within aes_wrapper is conclusively evidenced.

finding.summary: AES key material readable back via register interface without access control, and secure key store allows arbitrary writes that persist across reset

evidence.description: The read-side case statement (lines 128-139) exposes all 192 bits of key_big (the AES key) unconditionally through register addresses 16-21 via external_bus_io.rdata. No privilege check, lock bit, or access-control guard conditions the readback of key material. Any bus master with access to the AES wrapper's register window can read the full AES key.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material is readable back through the register interface without any access control guard.

evidence.description: key_big holds the full 192-bit AES key supplied via key_in. It is exposed at register addresses 16-21 as rdata without restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key is the sensitive resource being exposed.

evidence.description: In rom2, the always_ff block at line 40 allows writes to secure_reg (which stores all AES/JTAG/access-control keys) when we_i is asserted and req_i is high. There is no privilege level check, no write-once locking mechanism, and no authentication. Any bus master that can drive we_i and req_i can overwrite key slots including the AES key (index 0), JTAG key (index 2), and access-control keys (indices 1 and 3).
evidence.supports_claim: The secure key store allows unauthenticated arbitrary writes to all key slots.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni=0), secure_reg is restored from the const ROM (mem). However, after reset, writes are freely accepted without any lock-after-reset mechanism. The reset behavior is correct but provides no post-reset write protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset restores keys but provides no write-lock protection afterward.

evidence.description: secure_reg contains all four 192-bit key slots: AES key (index 0), JTAG key (index 2), and access-control keys (indices 1 and 3). The output secure_reg is also directly wired out as an output port.
evidence.supports_claim: The most sensitive assets in the system (all cryptographic and access-control keys) reside in secure_reg.

evidence.description: rom2 is instantiated with a direct bus interface (req_i, we_i, addr_i, wdata_i) connected to rom2_we, rom2_req, rom2_addr, rom2_wdata signals driven from a bus bridge. The bus master can freely assert write transactions with no hardware-enforced privilege filtering shown.
evidence.supports_claim: The entry point to the writable key store has no hardware access-control filter.

evidence.description: The AES wrapper is instantiated with key_in connected to key_reg_out[0], and external_bus_io connected to reg_bus_aes which is driven by a register bus. The read-side of the bus is unguarded, enabling key readback.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES wrapper's register bus allows unguarded key readback.

evidence.description: The write-side always block checks rst_ni and external_bus_io.write, and the read-side uses always @(*) with only address-based case dispatch. No privilege register, lock bit, or secure-mode flag gates access to key registers at addresses 16-21.
evidence.supports_claim: Absence of any guard logic for key register readback confirms the vulnerability.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete permission violations exist. First, the AES wrapper's combinational read path (SNIP-0001, lines 128-139) exposes all 192 bits of the AES key at register addresses 16-21 with no access-control guard; any read to those addresses returns key material directly. Second, rom2 (SNIP-0002, line 40) permits writes to secure_reg (holding AES, JTAG, and access-control keys) conditioned only on req_i and we_i with no privilege check, authentication, or write-once locking. The reset behavior copies the ROM contents to secure_reg but provides no post-reset write protection. Together these allow an unprivileged bus master to both read all AES key material and corrupt any key stored in the secure register file.
finding.security_impact: A malicious or compromised bus master can (1) exfiltrate the full 192-bit AES session key by reading register addresses 16-21, and (2) overwrite any entry in the secure key store including JTAG authentication keys and peripheral access-control keys, enabling privilege escalation, persistent authentication bypass, or forcing incorrect AES keys to break confidentiality.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The upstream bus arbitration/filtering logic (rom2 bus bridge and AES APB bridge) is not shown in the snippets; it is possible that software-visible access controls exist at the interconnect level. However, no hardware-enforced guard is visible in the RTL of aes_wrapper or rom2 themselves, and the finding is based solely on source_snippets as required.

finding.summary: The rom2 module stores all cryptographic keys and access-control data in a writable register array (secure_reg). Any bus master with write access to the rom2_fuse AXI slave can overwrite keys and access-control permissions at runtime with no authentication or privilege check beyond the standard AXI bus arbitration.

evidence.description: rom2.secure_reg is declared as an output port carrying all four 192-bit key slots (AES key, JTAG key, and two access-control bitmaps). On reset it is loaded from the constant mem array, but it remains writable at runtime via the we_i/req_i bus interface with no privilege or authentication guard.
evidence.supports_claim: secure_reg holds all sensitive keys and permission registers, and it is unconditionally writable through the bus interface.

evidence.description: Lines 36-41: when req_i is asserted and we_i is high, secure_reg[addr] <= wdata_i is executed without any check on the requester's privilege level, a lock bit, or any other authentication mechanism. An unprivileged bus master can therefore overwrite any key slot or access-control entry.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: no privilege/auth check before write to secure_reg.

evidence.description: Lines 215-217: access_ctrl_reg[0] and access_ctrl_reg[1] are directly driven from key_reg_out[2] and key_reg_out[3], which are the live contents of rom2.secure_reg. Because secure_reg is writable at runtime, the access-control registers fed to the AXI crossbar can be manipulated by any bus master that can reach the rom2_fuse slave.
evidence.supports_claim: Access-control permission state is derived without protection from a writable source.

evidence.description: Lines 448-452: access_ctrl[i][j] is unpacked directly from access_ctrl_reg without any secondary validation, lock, or immutability check before being forwarded to the AXI crossbar. No guard is interposed between the writable rom2 output and the crossbar permission inputs.
evidence.supports_claim: No guard between writable permission register and crossbar enforcement point.

evidence.description: key_reg_out[0] (the AES key slot from rom2.secure_reg) is fed directly to the AES wrapper as key_in. An attacker who can write to rom2 via the bus can substitute an arbitrary AES key, breaking confidentiality of all data encrypted by that engine.
evidence.supports_claim: Overwriting secure_reg allows key substitution attack on the AES engine.

evidence.description: jtag_key is driven from key_reg_out[1][31:0]. A bus-writable secure_reg allows an attacker to replace the JTAG authentication key, potentially enabling unauthorized JTAG access.
evidence.supports_claim: Overwriting secure_reg allows JTAG key substitution.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 is described as the key storage module ('ROM2: Which have all the keys'). However, its secure_reg output is not truly read-only after reset: the always_ff block unconditionally accepts writes (req_i && we_i) with no privilege check, no write-lock mechanism, and no authentication. The contents of secure_reg flow directly to (a) the AES encryption key input, (b) the JTAG authentication key output, and (c) the two access-control permission registers that gate the AXI crossbar. A bus master that can reach the rom2_fuse AXI slave can therefore arbitrarily rewrite keys and permissions, bypassing the entire permission enforcement model.
finding.security_impact: Any bus master with access to the rom2_fuse AXI slave can: (1) substitute an arbitrary AES session key, compromising data confidentiality; (2) replace the JTAG key to bypass debug authentication; (3) rewrite access-control bitmaps to grant itself or deny other masters peripheral access through the AXI crossbar. This constitutes a complete privilege-escalation and key-compromise vulnerability.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed from the available snippets whether the rom2_fuse AXI slave is memory-mapped into an address range reachable only by privileged bus masters or whether additional bus-fabric access controls exist upstream. However, the absence of any in-module write guard is clearly evidenced by the source code, and the sensitivity of the data being written is unambiguous.

finding.summary: Disabled peripheral stubs (SPI and Ethernet) respond to all AXI transactions with RESP_SLVERR but also return a hardcoded 'deadbeef' read data value, accepting every request unconditionally with aw_ready/ar_ready/w_ready permanently asserted high. This means any bus master that issues a transaction to these disabled peripherals receives an immediate successful handshake and a fixed data pattern, with no gating or access denial beyond the error response code. Additionally, the testharness instantiates ariane_peripherals with InclSPI=0 and InclEthernet=0, confirming these stub paths are always active in the testbench context.

evidence.description: When SPI is not included (else branch), spi.aw_ready, ar_ready, and w_ready are permanently driven 1'b1, unconditionally accepting all AXI transactions. The read data bus is driven with 'hdeadbeef and r_valid follows ar_valid, meaning any read transaction is immediately completed with a fixed data value. Only the response code (RESP_SLVERR) signals an error; the handshake itself is never blocked.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing access guard: disabled SPI peripheral accepts all AXI transactions unconditionally, returning a hardcoded data value rather than gating or ignoring the request.

evidence.description: The same pattern applies to the Ethernet stub: ethernet.aw_ready, ar_ready, w_ready are permanently 1'b1, ethernet.r_data is driven with 'hdeadbeef, and r_valid/b_valid follow ar_valid/aw_valid respectively, with only RESP_SLVERR as the error indicator. No actual blocking of access occurs.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing access guard: disabled Ethernet peripheral accepts all AXI transactions unconditionally, returning a hardcoded data value rather than gating or ignoring the request.

evidence.description: The testharness instantiates ariane_peripherals with InclSPI=1'b0 and InclEthernet=1'b0, confirming the stub paths with unconditional ready signals and deadbeef readback are always active in this testbench configuration.
evidence.supports_claim: The stub behavior with missing guards is permanently enabled in the testharness, not just an edge case.

evidence.description: The AXI_BUS.in ports for spi and ethernet are connected as peripheral slave interfaces; in a security-sensitive design, these should reject or not acknowledge transactions when the peripheral is disabled, rather than silently accepting with a fixed data response.
evidence.supports_claim: SPI and Ethernet AXI slave ports are the protected resources that should not accept transactions when the peripheral is absent.

finding.reasoning_summary: When InclSPI=0 or InclEthernet=0, the RTL stubs drive the AXI ready signals unconditionally high and respond to all read transactions with a fixed 0xdeadbeef data value, with r_valid and b_valid directly following the request valid signals. The only error indication is RESP_SLVERR in the response field, but the handshake proceeds immediately. This means any AXI master that targets these address ranges will complete transactions without any actual blocking. In a real security context this creates a scenario where an attacker can probe disabled peripheral address space, receive immediate acknowledgement and a predictable data pattern, without the bus fabric ever stalling or denying the transaction at the handshake level. The testharness confirms these stubs are always active.
finding.security_impact: A bus master can freely issue AXI read and write transactions to the disabled SPI and Ethernet peripheral address regions and receive immediate unconditional handshakes with a predictable 0xdeadbeef read data response. This bypasses any expectation that disabled peripherals would block or ignore accesses, potentially enabling information leakage (the fixed data pattern reveals peripheral configuration state) and undermining address-space isolation in the testbench environment.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: These stubs are in testbench files (tb/), so their impact on production RTL is limited. However, if this pattern is replicated in synthesizable RTL or if the testbench is used for security validation, the missing access guard is a real concern. No source evidence shows whether an upstream address decoder would prevent routing to these stubs in a real system, and no permission/privilege check source is present in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: Cryptographic keys stored in rom2 are directly readable via the AXI bus interface without any access control enforcement at the register read path, and the secure_reg output (containing all keys) is exposed as an unguarded output port that is wired to downstream logic including JTAG key and AES key inputs.

evidence.description: rom2 stores all cryptographic keys in a const memory and copies them to secure_reg on reset. The module accepts external write access (we_i) to secure_reg entries with no privilege check: 'secure_reg[addr_i[...]] <= wdata_i' is executed whenever req_i is asserted and we_i is high, with no access control guard.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master with access to the rom2 AXI region can overwrite key material in secure_reg without any authentication or permission check.

evidence.description: The always_ff block at line 32-44 has no privilege, lock, or lifecycle check gating writes. The only condition is req_i && we_i, which any AXI master can assert.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard on write path allows unauthenticated key overwrite.

evidence.description: Line 47: 'assign rdata_o = (raddr_q < RomSize) ? secure_reg[raddr_q] : 0;' exposes the full 192-bit key values from secure_reg to any bus master that reads from this address range. There is no read access control check.
evidence.supports_claim: Key material is readable by any AXI bus master without access control, enabling sensitive key readback.

evidence.description: Line 215: 'assign jtag_key = key_reg_out[1][31:0]' directly exposes 32 bits of the JTAG key from secure_reg to the jtag_key output port with no conditional guard or privilege check.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG key is unconditionally exposed as an output, creating a sensitive readback path.

evidence.description: Lines 216-217: access_ctrl_reg[0] and access_ctrl_reg[1] are assigned directly from key_reg_out[2] and key_reg_out[3] with no access control guard, exposing access control key material.
evidence.supports_claim: Access control key material is unconditionally driven as output without permission enforcement.

evidence.description: Line 467: 'key_in (key_reg_out[0])' feeds the AES key directly from rom2's secure_reg into the AES wrapper with no intermediary access control check. If key_reg_out[0] is overwritten via the unguarded write path, the AES engine will use attacker-controlled key material.
evidence.supports_claim: An attacker who overwrites secure_reg via the unguarded bus write path can control the AES encryption key.

evidence.description: jtag_key is declared as a plain logic signal in ariane_testharness and connected directly to ariane_peripherals without any filtering, masking, or access control logic in the testharness.
evidence.supports_claim: The JTAG key propagates from the key store to the top-level testharness with no protection.

evidence.description: ariane_peripherals is instantiated in the testharness with jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg as direct outputs, confirming the key material reaches the top-level hierarchy without restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: Key material is accessible at the top-level testbench hierarchy with no access control.

finding.reasoning_summary: rom2 stores all cryptographic keys and copies them to secure_reg on reset. The secure_reg can be overwritten by any AXI bus master with no privilege or lifecycle check (missing guard on write path). Additionally, secure_reg contents are directly readable via rdata_o and are unconditionally driven to output ports jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg with no access control conditions. The AES engine receives its key directly from this unguarded store. Both the read and write paths to the key store lack any permission enforcement.
finding.security_impact: An unprivileged AXI bus master can: (1) read all cryptographic key material including the JTAG key and AES key via the rdata_o read path; (2) overwrite any key entry in secure_reg, causing the AES engine to encrypt with attacker-controlled keys and potentially corrupting access control policy registers. This fully compromises the confidentiality and integrity of the system's key management.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is unclear whether there is AXI address-space isolation or bus firewall logic upstream of the rom2_fuse AXI port that would restrict which masters can access the rom2 address region. However, no such guard is visible in the provided source snippets, and the module itself has no internal access control.

finding.summary: The AES key stored in rom2's secure_reg is readable back through the AES wrapper's bus interface without any access control check, exposing the full 192-bit AES key to any bus master that can read addresses 16–21 of the AES peripheral register space.

evidence.description: rom2 stores cryptographic keys in a const memory array and copies them into 'secure_reg' on reset. The secure_reg output (key_reg_out) holds all four 192-bit key slots including the AES key at index 0.
evidence.supports_claim: The AES key originates from a dedicated secure key store (rom2/secure_reg) intended to protect key material.

evidence.description: key_reg_out[0] (the AES key) is passed directly to aes_wrapper.key_in at line 467, and key_reg_out[2][47:0] and key_reg_out[3][47:0] are assigned to access_ctrl_reg without any gating or masking.
evidence.supports_claim: The secret key value flows unprotected from rom2 into the AES wrapper's key_in port.

evidence.description: aes_wrapper is instantiated with key_in connected directly to key_reg_out[0], making the full 192-bit key available inside the wrapper. No permission check gates this connection.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard between the key storage and the AES wrapper's internal key register.

evidence.description: The read-side case statement in aes_wrapper covers addresses 0–25 via always @(*) with no write-enable or privilege check. There is no condition preventing reads when external_bus_io.write is asserted or based on any access_ctrl_reg value.
evidence.supports_claim: The read path has no permission guard; any bus transaction to the read addresses returns data unconditionally.

evidence.description: Register addresses 16–21 in the AES wrapper's read case directly return key_big[191:0] (the full 192-bit AES key) in 32-bit chunks to external_bus_io.rdata with no access control check, privilege level check, or lock bit.
evidence.supports_claim: Any bus master can read back the complete AES key by issuing six reads to addresses 16–21, bypassing all access controls.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg is derived from rom2 key slots 2 and 3 and distributed to the AXI crossbar as peripheral access permissions, but the aes_wrapper read path does not consult access_ctrl or priv_lvl before returning key material.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control infrastructure exists but is not applied to protect key readback from the AES peripheral registers.

finding.reasoning_summary: The rom2 module holds a 192-bit AES key in secure_reg[0]. This key is exported as key_reg_out[0] and wired to aes_wrapper.key_in. Inside aes_wrapper, key_big is assigned from key_in (line 57) and then returned verbatim to external_bus_io.rdata at register offsets 16–21 (lines 129–139) whenever those addresses are read. The always @(*) read-side block has no check on privilege level, no lock bit, and no use of access_ctrl_reg. Although an access_ctrl matrix is computed from other key slots and fed to an AXI crossbar, it is not used to gate reads of the key registers inside aes_wrapper. finding.summary: Hardcoded privilege-level bypass: manager index 6 always inherits manager index 7's access permissions regardless of privilege level, allowing unauthorized access to subordinate resources.

evidence.description: Line 430 contains a hardcoded special case: `assign connectivity_map_o[i][j] = access_ctrl_i[i][j][priv_lvl_i] || ((j==6) && access_ctrl_i[i][7][priv_lvl_i]);`. When j==6, the connectivity map grants access if EITHER manager 6 has permission OR manager 7 has permission at the current privilege level. This means manager 6 automatically inherits all of manager 7's access rights across all privilege levels and all subordinates, bypassing the intended per-manager access control policy.

evidence.description: The connectivity_mapping module's only guard logic is the access_ctrl_i matrix indexed by priv_lvl_i. finding.reasoning_summary: The `connectivity_mapping` module generates a per-subordinate-per-manager connectivity bitmap from an access control matrix indexed by privilege level. However, line 430 contains a hardcoded special case where manager index 6's connectivity bit is set if either manager 6 OR manager 7 has access at the current privilege level. This means manager 6 can never be more restricted than manager 7 — any access permitted to manager 7 is automatically granted to manager 6. This is a structural bypass of the access control policy: an operator configuring access_ctrl_i to deny manager 6 access to a subordinate at a given privilege level would be unaware that manager 7's permissions silently override that denial. The hardcoding of indices 6 and 7 further suggests this is not a parameterized design intent but an undocumented or erroneous special case.
finding.security_impact: Any AXI subordinate (slave) that should be inaccessible to manager 6 at a given privilege level can be accessed if manager 7 has permission at that same privilege level. This undermines compartmentalization and least-privilege enforcement in the AXI interconnect, potentially allowing a lower-privileged or untrusted bus master (index 6) to access memory regions or peripherals it should not reach.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The intended design rationale for the j==6/j==7 special case is not documented in the provided snippets. finding.summary: AES 192-bit key material stored in rom2.secure_reg is fully readable via the external bus interface of aes_wrapper without any access-control or privilege check, allowing any bus master to exfiltrate the cryptographic key.

evidence.description: rom2.secure_reg holds all four 192-bit key slots loaded from fuse memory on reset (line 34: secure_reg <= mem). The comment on line 2 explicitly states 'ROM2: Which have all the keys.' The register is also writable via the bus when req_i && we_i (line 40), further weakening key protection.
evidence.supports_claim: Establishes that secure_reg is the root store for sensitive key material, including the AES key at index [0].

evidence.description: key_reg_out (connected to rom2.secure_reg) is forwarded with no filtering: jtag_key = key_reg_out[1][31:0] (line 215), access_ctrl_reg[0] = key_reg_out[2][47:0] (line 216), access_ctrl_reg[1] = key_reg_out[3][47:0] (line 217). The AES key key_reg_out[0] is passed directly to aes_wrapper.key_in (line 467 via SNIP-0007). No masking or gating is applied.
evidence.supports_claim: Shows that the full content of secure_reg is distributed to outputs and sub-modules without access control enforcement.

evidence.description: Line 57: 'assign key_big = key_in;' — the 192-bit AES key is unconditionally wire-assigned to key_big. The always block that handles writes (lines 61–100) resets internal AES operands on ~rst_ni but never restricts readability of key_big. No privilege-level or access-control signal gates this assignment.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms the absence of any guard preventing key_big from being read back through the bus interface.

evidence.description: The read-side combinational block at lines 129–139 maps external_bus_io.rdata to key_big[191:160..31:0] for register addresses 16–21 with no conditional check on caller privilege, access_ctrl_reg, or any other permission signal. Any bus master that can reach this register offset receives the complete 192-bit AES key in six 32-bit reads.
evidence.supports_claim: Directly demonstrates the sensitive key readback vulnerability: the full AES key is exposed unconditionally through the memory-mapped register interface.

evidence.description: aes_wrapper is instantiated with key_in connected to key_reg_out[0] (line 467), providing the path from rom2 key storage to the aes_wrapper register map that leaks the key.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms the data-flow entry point: rom2 secure_reg slot 0 flows directly into the aes_wrapper where it becomes readable.

evidence.description: access_ctrl_reg (derived from rom2 key slots 2 and 3) is used to populate per-peripheral access control bits in the AXI interconnect (line 450: access_ctrl[i][j] = access_ctrl_reg[i][4*j +: 4]). Since secure_reg is writable without authentication (SNIP-0003 line 40), an attacker can also corrupt access control policy for all peripherals in addition to reading the AES key.
evidence.supports_claim: Demonstrates broader security impact: key exfiltration and potential access control policy tampering through the same unguarded interface.

finding.reasoning_summary: The chain from rom2.secure_reg through key_reg_out to aes_wrapper.key_in and finally to external_bus_io.rdata is entirely unguarded. SNIP-0001 shows key_big is a direct wire assignment from key_in. SNIP-0002 shows key_big is split across six read-register addresses with no privilege check, access_ctrl check, or any conditional guard. SNIP-0003 additionally shows secure_reg is write-accessible via the bus, enabling key corruption and policy tampering. Together these constitute a concrete sensitive-readback and missing-guard violation.
finding.security_impact: Any bus master with access to the aes_wrapper register space can read the full 192-bit AES key in six sequential 32-bit register reads. Additionally, writes to rom2 via the unguarded we_i path can corrupt cryptographic keys and overwrite the access control policy bits that govern all peripheral access decisions in the AXI interconnect.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not confirmed whether the AXI interconnect enforces any upstream privilege check before allowing accesses to the aes_wrapper address range; if it does, the exploitability would be reduced to privileged software. However, the absence of any guard within aes_wrapper itself is unambiguous from the source snippets provided.
